lesson.title: Functions I: why, define, and call
masteryOutcomes: Understand how functions reduce repetition and improve reuse.
Understand the structure of Python functions.
Define a function with one or two parameters and call it with literals and variables.

concept: Defining and calling Python functions with parameters to reuse code instead of repeating it
prerequisites: Reading a traceback to find what went wrong

explanations.text: A function is like a recipe you write once and use whenever you want. Instead of copying the same steps over and over, you give the steps a name — then you just say the name and the computer does all the steps. If the recipe needs ingredients, you pass them in when you call it.

You define a function with def, a name, parentheses, and a colon, then indent the body. Parameters are named inputs inside the parentheses. Nothing runs until you call the function by writing its name with values in parentheses. For example: def greet(name): print("Hello, " + name) — then greet("Sam") runs the body with name set to "Sam". Calling it again with a different value reuses the same code with new input.

def creates a function object and binds it to a name; the indented block is its body and only executes on a call. Parameters like name in def greet(name): are local variables that receive the arguments you pass at the call site — greet("Sam") passes the literal "Sam", and greet(friend) passes whatever the variable friend refers to. Order matters for positional arguments: in def repeat(word, times):, the first argument fills word and the second fills times. If you call a function before its def has run, or misspell its name, Python raises a NameError; passing the wrong number of arguments raises a TypeError — and the traceback points you at the call that caused it.

examples:
def greet(name):
    print("Hello, " + name)

greet("Sam")
greet("Ana")

hintLadder.text: A function definition starts with a special keyword. Which three-letter word tells Python you are about to define a function?
After def comes the function name, then parentheses holding the parameter names, then a colon at the end of the line.
The body — the lines the function runs — must be indented under the def line. Try indenting with four spaces.
Defining is not running. After the def block, add a separate line that calls the function: its name followed by parentheses with a value inside.

misconceptions.trigger: Defines the function correctly but nothing appears in the output, because they never call it.
misconceptions.correction: A def only teaches Python the recipe — it doesn't cook it. Add a call after the definition, like greet("Sam"), and run again to see output.

misconceptions.trigger: Forgets the colon or the indentation, e.g. def greet(name) with the body flush left.
misconceptions.correction: The def line must end with a colon, and every line of the body must be indented under it. Python uses that indentation to know what belongs to the function.

misconceptions.trigger: Calls the function with quotes around the whole call, e.g. print("greet(Sam)"), or puts the call inside a string.
misconceptions.correction: A call is code, not text. Write greet("Sam") on its own line — only the argument is a string in quotes, not the whole call.

misconceptions.trigger: Confuses parameters and arguments, e.g. writes def greet("Sam"): with a literal in the def line.
misconceptions.correction: In the def line you write parameter names — placeholders like name — without quotes. The actual values (arguments) go in the call: greet("Sam").

misconceptions.trigger: Swaps argument order in a two-parameter function, e.g. repeat(3, "ha") for def repeat(word, times).
misconceptions.correction: Positional arguments fill parameters in order: the first argument becomes word, the second becomes times. Match the order in the def line — repeat("ha", 3).

misconceptions.trigger: Believes functions are only for 'big' programs and copy-pasting is fine for now.
misconceptions.correction: Copy-paste breaks the moment you need a change: you must fix every copy. A function is one place to change — edit the body once and every call is updated.

checks.prompt: Define a function called greet that takes one parameter, name, and prints Hello, followed by that name. Then call it twice with two different names.
acceptanceCriteria: Uses def greet(name): with a colon and an indented body
The body uses print() and includes the name parameter in the output
Contains at least two calls to greet with string arguments after the definition
Running it prints two different greetings without errors
checks.passFeedback: You defined a function once and reused it twice — that's the whole point of functions, working.
checks.failFeedback: Check three things: def greet(name): ends in a colon, the print line is indented under it, and you call greet("...") on unindented lines afterward.

checks.prompt: Define a function repeat(word, times) that prints the word repeated that many times (hint: word * times). Call it once with literals, and once passing a variable as the word.
acceptanceCriteria: Defines repeat with exactly two parameters, word and times
One call uses literal arguments, e.g. repeat("ha", 3)
One call passes a variable as the first argument, e.g. w = "hi" then repeat(w, 2)
Runs without errors and prints the repeated text
checks.passFeedback: Two parameters, called with both literals and a variable — you've got the full define-and-call pattern.
checks.failFeedback: Make sure the def line lists both parameters, the call gives the word first and the number second, and one call uses a variable instead of a quoted string.

practice.prompt: Write a function welcome(name, city) that prints a message like: Welcome to Paris, Maya! Then call it with two different name/city pairs, and once using variables for both arguments.
practice.starterCode: # Define welcome(name, city) below, then call it.
acceptanceCriteria: Defines welcome with two parameters using def, parentheses, and a colon
The body is indented and uses print() with both parameters in the message
Calls welcome at least twice with different literal arguments
At least one call passes variables as arguments
Runs without errors and prints the expected messages

practice.prompt: You have the same three print lines copy-pasted five times in a program, and now you need to change the wording. In your own words, how would a function help here?
rubric: Says the repeated lines can become one function defined once
Says each copy becomes a short call to that function
Notes the wording change now happens in one place (the body) and all calls get the update

practice.prompt: Given def greet(name): with body print("Hi, " + name), which line correctly calls it?
practice.explanation: A call is the function name with parentheses, and text arguments need quotes. greet name has no parentheses, def starts a definition (not a call), and greet(Ana) without quotes makes Python look for a variable named Ana and raise a NameError.

pedagogyRules: Always separate the two steps out loud: first define, then call. If output is missing, ask 'did you call it?' before anything else.
When a call errors, have the student read the traceback themselves — NameError means misspelled or not-yet-defined, TypeError means wrong number of arguments — and connect it to last lesson's skill.
Never fix indentation for the student; ask them which lines should belong to the function body and let them indent.
If the student says they already know functions, skip the explanation and go straight to the two-parameter check; advance on a clean run.
In challenge mode, guide with one hint-ladder step at a time and let the student run between hints; do not show a full working def.
When motivation dips, point at concrete repetition in their own code and ask which lines they'd hate to copy five times.
Keep return values out of scope: if asked about return, say it's next lesson, give a one-line preview, and steer back to defining and calling.

failurePlaybooks.correctMove: Only confirm success when the student's run actually prints the expected output. A def with no call produces no output — that is not a pass; ask them to add a call and run again.
Trust the real interpreter output. If the traceback says IndentationError, name indentation; if NameError, name the misspelled or missing definition; never invent a different cause.
If the student is lost, drop back to a one-parameter function they define and call before attempting two parameters; one new idea per run.

failurePlaybooks.failureMode: Student wants to skip or jump ahead (e.g. to return values or loops)
failurePlaybooks.correctMove: Acknowledge the curiosity, give a one-sentence preview, then require one clean run of a two-parameter function call before moving on.

chunks.text: Functions exist to kill repetition. If you write the same lines of code in several places, you must fix every copy when something changes. A function lets you write those lines once, give them a name, and reuse them anywhere by calling the name. One change in the function body updates every place that calls it.

A Python function definition has four parts: the keyword def, the function name, parentheses holding zero or more parameter names, and a colon. The body — the lines the function runs — is indented underneath. Example:

def greet(name):
    print("Hello, " + name)

Defining a function does not run it; the body only executes when the function is called.

You call a function by writing its name with parentheses containing arguments — the actual values for its parameters. greet("Sam") passes the literal string "Sam", so inside the body name is "Sam". You can also pass a variable: friend = "Maya" then greet(friend) passes the value "Maya". Each call runs the body fresh with the new input.

With two parameters, arguments fill parameters in order (positional). Example:

def repeat(word, times):
    print(word * times)

repeat("ha", 3) prints hahaha because word becomes "ha" and times becomes 3. Swapping them — repeat(3, "ha") — still runs here because Python multiplies either way, but in general order matters: the first argument goes to the first parameter.

Common function errors and their tracebacks: calling a function before its def has run, or misspelling its name, raises NameError. Forgetting the colon raises SyntaxError. A body that is not indented raises IndentationError. Calling with the wrong number of arguments — greet() when the def has one parameter — raises TypeError saying a required argument is missing. The traceback's last line names the error type; the arrow above it points at the offending line.

celebration.headline: You wrote your first reusable function.
celebration.recap: You defined functions with def, gave them one and two parameters, and called them with both literals and variables — writing code once and reusing it on demand.
celebration.unlocked: Next: functions that hand a result back with return, so you can use their answers in more code.

summaryBullets: You can now explain why one function beats five copy-pasted blocks: one body to edit, many calls that all update.
You can now write a complete function definition — def, name, parameters, colon, and an indented body.
You can now call a function with literal values like greet("Sam") and with variables like greet(friend).
You can now define and call a two-parameter function, keeping the arguments in the right order.
You can now use a traceback to tell a missing call, a misspelled name, and a wrong argument count apart.
openingLine: {name}, ever copy-pasted the same lines of code three times and thought 'there has to be a better way'? There is — it's called a function, and today you'll write your own. Ready to see how one line can replace five?
bridgeFromPreviousLesson: Last time you learned to read tracebacks and pinpoint what went wrong — today that skill pays off immediately, because functions come with their own classic errors, and you'll be able to decode every one of them.

dialogueExemplars.title: Wrong but confident: defining means running

turns.text: I wrote def greet(name): print("Hello, " + name) and ran it. It's done — but the output is empty, so print must be broken.
Your def is actually perfect — nice work on the colon and indentation. But a def only teaches Python the recipe; it never cooks it. What line could you add after the definition to actually run it?
Oh... maybe greet("Sam") on a new line?
Exactly — that's a call, and calls are what make the body run. Type it under your def and press Run. What shows up in the output?

dialogueExemplars.title: Why do I even need functions?

turns.text: Honestly, why do I need this? I can just copy-paste my print lines wherever I want them.

Fair question — copy-paste works until something changes. Imagine a game that prints a 3-line welcome banner in ten places, and the studio renames the game: with copy-paste you fix ten spots, with a function you fix one body and all ten calls update. Want to feel that yourself?
Tiny task: write def banner(): with two indented print lines, call it three times, and run. Then change one word inside the body and run again — how many places did you have to edit?
Just one! All three banners changed.
That's the whole superpower: write once, reuse everywhere, change in one place. Ready to add a parameter so the banner can greet different players?

dialogueExemplars.title: Swapped arguments in a two-parameter call

turns.text: I wrote def welcome(name, city): print("Welcome to " + city + ", " + name + "!") and called welcome("Paris", "Maya") — but it printed Welcome to Maya, Paris! That's backwards.

Good catch reading your own output — the code ran, it just got the wrong ingredients. Arguments fill parameters in order: your first argument lands in name and your second lands in city. Looking at your def line, which value should come first in the call?
Exactly right. Order in the call must match the order in the def line. Swap them in the editor and press Run — does Maya get welcomed to Paris now?
